params.text:
Recording it properly

A report is only useful if it is clear and honest. Whether you use a paper accident book, an incident form or a digital system, follow the same principles.

  • Record factually — write what you saw and did, not opinions or guesses about blame.
  • Be accurate — include who, what, when, where and any witnesses.
  • Report promptly — while the details are fresh, ideally before the end of your shift.
  • Sign and date the record so it is clear who completed it and when.

Describe injuries and actions plainly, for example: “Mrs A was found sitting on the floor beside her bed at 14:10. She said she felt dizzy. No visible injury. First aider called, GP informed.”

params.taskDescription: Drag the words into the gaps to complete these good-practice rules for recording an accident or incident.
params.textField: Always record what happened *factually*, sticking to the facts and not to opinions about blame. Make sure your account is *accurate*, including who, what, when and where. Complete the report *promptly*, before details are forgotten. Finally, *sign* and date the entry so it is clear who wrote it, and keep the information *confidential*, sharing it only with those who need it.
